steps.title: Determine the slope direction
steps.content: Examine the scatterplot for the general trend. If the points fall from upper-left to lower-right, the data has a negative correlation and the line of best fit has a negative slope. The two options with negative slopes are A ($y=-1.9x-10.1$) and B ($y=-1.9x+10.1$).

steps.title: Determine the y-intercept sign
steps.content: Locate where the trend would cross the y-axis (at $x=0$). A line with a positive y-intercept crosses above the x-axis; a negative y-intercept crosses below. For a downward trend whose left-side $y$ values are clearly positive, the y-intercept must be positive: $b=+10.1$.

steps.title: Combine slope and intercept
steps.content: A negative slope of $-1.9$ paired with a positive y-intercept of $+10.1$ gives $$y=-1.9x+10.1.$$

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• A) $y=-1.9x-10.1$ — correct negative slope, but the y-intercept is negative. Eliminate.
  • B) $y=-1.9x+10.1$ — negative slope and positive y-intercept match the downward trend. Keep.
  • C) $y=1.9x-10.1$ — positive slope contradicts the downward trend. Eliminate.
  • D) $y=1.9x+10.1$ — positive slope contradicts the downward trend. Eliminate.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
